metadata.title: Sell the Solution & the Magic, Simplified (Slides 3–4)
metadata.playlist: Sell the Vision — Your Investor Pitch Video
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 brief — Video Pitch to Investors: Slides 3–4 (solution + magic)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 3 of 7. Slides 3 and 4 are the aha moment. Slide 3 says: here is what the world looks like after the problem is fixed. Slide 4 says: here is why that was not possible before — and here is the one thing that makes it work now. Together they take 2 minutes. Together they are the reason anyone would fund this over the alternatives. The failure mode for both slides is the same: going technical. Describing how the system works instead of what the system does. Listing features instead of the aha. Using words that require explaining. This episode covers how to write the solution in plain English, how to write the magic in one sentence a seventh-grader gets, and how to run the translation guide on your own slides — the jargon-audit pass that turns a developer's description into an investor's opportunity.

props.segment: Sell the Solution & the Magic, Simplified (Slides 3–4)
props.command: Write my solution and magic slides in plain English — then run the jargon-audit pass on both.
props.runningText: slides 3 and 4 are the aha moment — what the world looks like after the fix, and the one thing that makes it work now…
output: Slide 3: the aha in plain English — before/after, no jargon, 75 seconds
Slide 4: 'We use AI to…' one sentence, seventh-grader level, 45 seconds
Jargon-audit pass: every technical term translated before recording

beats.narration_text: Slide 3 is the solution slide. Its job is to show the aha — the moment the investor understands how the problem ends. The structure that works is a before/after: what the user does now, and what the user does instead with your tool. 'Right now, an email marketing coordinator spends the first three hours of Monday compiling last week's campaign data from six different platforms into a report. With this tool, that report writes itself and lands in their inbox before they sit down.' That is a solution slide. It names the old behavior, names the new behavior, and implicitly shows the value. What a solution slide is not: a feature list. 'The tool has a real-time dashboard, automated data ingestion, AI-powered summarization, and a customizable reporting template' — that is a menu. The investor does not want a menu. They want the aha. Say the aha in one or two sentences. Show the before and after. The investor will ask about the features. That is a good sign.

props.artifactHeading: The aha in plain English — before/after
artifactLines: Structure: what the user does now vs what they do instead with your tool.
Example: 'Right now: 3 hours compiling from 6 platforms. With this tool: report writes itself and lands in their inbox.'
The solution slide shows the aha — not the feature list.
A feature list is a menu. Investors want the aha. They will ask about features if they're interested.
Test: read slide 3 aloud. Does the investor know exactly what changed for the user?
props.sparkLine: Show the before and the after. The aha is the gap between them.

beats.narration_text: Slide 4 is the magic slide. It answers one question: why did this not exist before, and what makes it possible now? The format is a single sentence starting with 'We use AI to...' followed by what that means in plain English. Not how it works — what it does. Google's PageRank magic was: 'We rank web pages by counting how many other pages link to them.' Not a description of the algorithm — the idea. For your tool, it might be: 'We use AI to read your campaign data automatically and write a plain-English summary, the way a very fast junior analyst would — but in seconds, not hours.' That is a magic sentence. It says what the AI does, gives the investor a mental model that requires no technical background, and connects back to the problem on slide 2. What a magic slide is not: 'We use transformer-based language models with attention mechanisms fine-tuned on marketing analytics data.' That is the architecture description. Save it for technical due diligence. The magic slide is for the imagination, not the spec sheet.

props.artifactHeading: 'We use AI to…' — one sentence, seventh-grader level
artifactLines: Formula: 'We use AI to [what it does in plain English] — [mental model analogy].'
Example: 'We use AI to read your campaign data and write a plain-English summary — like a very fast junior analyst, but in seconds.'
PageRank model: describe the idea, not the algorithm. Mental model first.
Wrong: 'transformer-based LLMs fine-tuned on marketing analytics data.' → architecture, not magic.
Test: would a seventh-grader understand what the AI does from this one sentence?
props.sparkLine: Describe the idea. Save the architecture for due diligence.

beats.act: the translation guide — jargon to plain English
beats.narration_text: The translation guide is the most important editing tool for slides 3 and 4 — and for the whole pitch. Here is the core list. 'n8n workflow' becomes 'an automated system that connects your tools.' 'Multi-agent orchestration' becomes 'multiple AI assistants working in sequence.' 'API call' becomes 'a request to a data service.' 'Vector database' becomes 'a smart search index.' 'LLM inference' becomes 'the AI generating a response.' 'Prompt engineering' becomes 'writing clear instructions for the AI.' 'Data pipeline' becomes 'the steps that move data from source to output.' For each technical term in your slides 3 and 4, ask: would my grandmother understand this? If the answer is no, use the translation. Some terms are unavoidable — 'AI' is fine, everyone knows it. 'Automation' is fine. But if you are explaining what automation means, you have already lost the room. Run the madison-pitch jargon-audit pass. It flags every term in your slides that a non-technical audience would need explained and suggests a plain-English replacement.

artifactLines: 'n8n workflow' → 'an automated system that connects your tools'
'Multi-agent orchestration' → 'multiple AI assistants working in sequence'
'API call' → 'a request to a data service'
'LLM inference' → 'the AI generating a response'
'Vector database' → 'a smart search index' · 'Prompt engineering' → 'clear instructions for the AI'
Test: would a non-technical family member understand every word on slides 3 and 4?
props.sparkLine: If a word needs explaining, it doesn't belong on the slide.

beats.narration_text: The traps that kill slides 3 and 4. First: technical jargon. Any word you would not use with a non-technical family member needs to come out or be translated. One jargon term in the solution slide signals to the investor that you do not know how to explain your own work. Second: a feature list on the solution slide. The investor does not want a menu. They want the aha. The aha is a change in someone's life — before and after. List the features in a follow-up conversation, not on slide 3. Third: a magic slide that only an engineer understands. If your magic sentence requires prior knowledge of transformer architecture, you have described the implementation instead of the idea. Rewrite it with an analogy. Fourth: no connection back to the problem. Slide 4 should feel like a direct answer to slide 2. If the problem was 'three hours of manual work every Monday,' the magic should feel like the thing that makes those three hours disappear. If it does not connect, re-read slides 2 and 4 back to back and ask what is missing.

props.artifactHeading: Four failures between the aha and the close
artifactLines: Trap 1: technical jargon. One jargon term signals you can't explain your own work. Translate everything.
Trap 2: feature list on slide 3. The aha is a change in someone's life — before and after. Not a menu.
Trap 3: magic only an engineer gets. Use an analogy. Describe the idea, not the implementation.
Trap 4: slides 3–4 don't connect to slide 2. The magic should feel like the thing that ends the problem.
Test: read slides 2, 3, 4 in sequence. Does each one follow from the last?
props.sparkLine: Slide 4 is a direct answer to slide 2. If it doesn't feel that way, rewrite one of them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 3. Slide 3: the aha in plain English, structured as a before and after. The old behavior and the new behavior. No feature list, no jargon. The investor should understand exactly what changed for the user. Slide 4: 'We use AI to...' followed by one plain-English sentence, seventh-grader level. An analogy is better than an architecture description. Connect it directly to the problem on slide 2. Jargon test: run the translation guide on every technical term on both slides. If any term would require explaining to a non-technical family member, it comes out. Trap: solving for the architecture instead of the idea. Trap: a feature list instead of the aha. The magic slide is for the imagination. The spec sheet comes later.

props.artifactTitle: Sell the Solution & the Magic, Simplified
artifactLines: Slide 3: before/after in plain English. The aha, not the feature list. No jargon.
Slide 4: 'We use AI to…' one sentence, analogy-level simplicity. Connects directly to slide 2.
Jargon test: every technical term translated before recording.
Trap: feature list on slide 3. Investors want the aha — features come in follow-up.
Trap: magic only an engineer understands. Analogy first; architecture in due diligence.
Connection test: read slides 2–3–4 in sequence. Each should follow from the last.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here is my Part 1 sentence and the aha from my tool: [paste it]. Here is the problem from slide 2: [paste it]. Write Slides 3 and 4. Slide 3: write the solution as a before/after. Old behavior, new behavior, plain English, no technical jargon. The aha should be obvious from the before/after without requiring additional explanation. Slide 4: write the magic as a single sentence starting with 'We use AI to...' — seventh-grader level, with an analogy if it helps. Connect it directly to the problem I described on slide 2. After writing both slides, run the jargon-audit pass: list every technical term on slides 3 and 4 and replace each with plain-English language that a non-technical investor would immediately understand. After the prompt runs: say slide 3's aha aloud. Does the before/after land? Is slide 4's one sentence something a seventh-grader would understand? If your magic sentence still requires a technical background — rewrite it with a different analogy.

props.segment: Sell the Solution & the Magic, Simplified (Slides 3–4)
props.command: Here is my Part 1 sentence / aha: [paste it]. Here is the problem from slide 2: [paste it]. Write Slides 3–4. Slide 3: before/after in plain English, no jargon — the aha should be obvious. Slide 4: 'We use AI to…' one sentence, seventh-grader level, connects to slide 2 problem. Then jargon-audit pass: list every technical term on both slides and replace each with plain-English language.
props.runningText: paste your Part 1 sentence and slide 2 problem before running — the solution only lands if it connects to the pain you already established…

beats.narration_text: Sell the Solution & the Magic, Simplified. Liam, in for Bear.
